metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a comparative baseline check to `prerequisite_fidelity_report` (e.g., accept an optional `baseline_recall`/`baseline_false_accept` parameter) and add a slice-2 AC plus a proving command that loads the pinned singleton baseline and asserts packed metrics meet the comparative floor, not only the absolute floor.
evidence: 'require packed recall to be at least the pinned singleton recall and packed false-acceptance to be no greater than the pinned singleton value'
'prerequisite_fidelity_report ... adds failures when candidate recall is below min_recall, candidate false acceptance exceeds max_false_accept' — no comparative-to-baseline check is defined
'Run .venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing; require exit 0' — CLI only runs the absolute-floor report, not a baseline comparison
findings.finding: The slice-2 comparative gate ('packed recall ≥ pinned singleton recall AND packed false-acceptance ≤ pinned singleton value') is stated in the implementation narrative but has no corresponding acceptance criterion, no automated enforcement in `prerequisite_fidelity_report`, and no proving command that would fail when packed quality degrades relative to the singleton baseline. The `fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing` exit-0 check only verifies the absolute 0.90/0.10 floors. An executor could ship batching that silently regresses relative to the singleton baseline and still satisfy every AC.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence, step 2 / Verification section / `prerequisite_fidelity_report` definition (step 3)
scenarios: Packed recall drops from 0.95 (singleton baseline) to 0.91 (still above 0.90 absolute floor); all ACs pass and slice-2 gate is considered met, but quality has regressed.
The singleton baseline is recorded in a ticket comment but never loaded by any automated check, making the comparative requirement purely honor-system.
findings.suggested_fix: In `prerequisite_fidelity_report`, add optional `baseline_recall: float | None = None` and `baseline_false_accept: float | None = None` parameters; when provided, add failures when `candidate_recall < baseline_recall` or `candidate_false_accept > baseline_false_accept`. Expose `--singleton-baseline-json <path>` in the CLI so the pinned baseline output (recorded in the ticket comment) can be fed back as input and the comparative check runs automatically. Add an AC: 'Packed fidelity report loaded with the pinned singleton baseline exits 0 with no comparative-regression failure.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit migration or rollback strategy for the `mint_finding_id` encoding change — either a dual-write period that emits both old and new IDs, a version prefix on the new IDs so consumers can detect the format change, or a documented cut-over procedure that acknowledges stored IDs will become stale.
evidence: 'Use structured finding identity rather than printable concatenation: hash length-prefixed UTF-8 fields... No pipe-delimited identity path remains.'
No mention of a migration script, dual-write period, version field in the ID, or consumer notification strategy.
findings.finding: `mint_finding_id` is changed from a pipe-delimited encoding to a structured length-prefixed SHA-256 encoding, and the plan states 'No pipe-delimited identity path remains.' Any downstream consumer (dashboards, deduplication stores, comment threads) that has stored or compared finding IDs generated under the old scheme will silently see all IDs change with no migration path described.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 5; Binding signal-chain and identity tests section
scenarios: A ticket already has comments referencing specific finding IDs from a prior review; after deployment those IDs no longer resolve, silently breaking traceability.
A deduplication or suppression store keyed on finding ID treats post-migration IDs as new findings, re-surfacing previously suppressed issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-byte version prefix (e.g. `v2:`) to new IDs so consumers can detect the format boundary without a full migration; document that pre-existing IDs are not forward-compatible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the verdict dict should look when `prerequisite_indeterminate` is True and blocking findings are also present — either clear `blocking` to `[]` (with findings moved to `indeterminate`), or document explicitly that `verdict='INDETERMINATE'` can coexist with a non-empty `blocking` array and update every downstream consumer accordingly.
evidence: Plan text: 'immediately after extracting partition lists and before the current `if blocking` branch, add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"`, with existing branches changed to `elif`'
Current `finalize_verdict` returns `{'verdict': verdict, ..., 'blocking': blocking, ...}` — if `verdict='INDETERMINATE'` but `blocking` is non-empty, the dict is internally inconsistent

The AC says 'The unchanged signing boundary signs only PASS' — consistent — but nothing prevents a consumer from checking `result['blocking']` and seeing findings while `result['verdict']` says INDETERMINATE
findings.finding: The plan modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` so that `if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` precedes `elif blocking:`, meaning a prerequisite-indeterminate suppresses a BLOCK verdict string even when `blocking` findings are present. The resulting verdict dict will have `verdict='INDETERMINATE'` with a non-empty `blocking` array — an internal inconsistency no consumer currently expects. Downstream consumers (signers, claim gates, CI integrations) that branch on `blocking` being empty or on the verdict string will see contradictory signals. The plan does not address how consumers of the verdict dict should interpret this combination.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`'; finalize_verdict in orchestrator.py lines 497-574
scenarios: A claim-gate consumer that checks `verdict == 'BLOCK' or len(result['blocking']) > 0` will fire on the blocking array while the verdict string says INDETERMINATE, causing duplicate or conflicting error messages
A UI renderer that gates the blocking-findings display on `verdict == 'BLOCK'` will hide blocking findings that are present

findings.finding: `PrerequisiteBlock` is used as a named type in `pack_prerequisite_bins`'s signature (`blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock]`) and the plan refers to `block.rendered_text` in the bin-charge formula, but the type's field schema is never defined anywhere in the plan. An executor cannot implement `pack_prerequisite_bins` or the focused finder route without knowing `PrerequisiteBlock`'s fields (at minimum: `canonical_id`, `rendered_text`; possibly also `plan_text`, `pinned_material`).
findings.location: Binding Sequence step 4 — `pack_prerequisite_bins(blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock], ...)` signature and `est_tokens(block.rendered_text)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or strengthen AC 14) that explicitly requires: (a) a singleton-only run passing the absolute thresholds, (b) its recall and false-acceptance values recorded as the pinned baseline before batching is implemented, and (c) the packed run's recall ≥ singleton baseline recall and packed false-acceptance ≤ singleton baseline false-acceptance.
evidence: Step 1: 'Run the singleton gold evaluation before implementing batching... Record this first green output as the pinned singleton baseline in a ticket comment.'
Step 2: 'require packed recall to be at least the pinned singleton recall and packed false-acceptance to be no greater than the pinned singleton value.'
AC 14: 'The opt-in prerequisite evaluation meets recall, false-acceptance, coverage, and attribution thresholds.' — no mention of two-phase ordering or singleton-baseline comparison.
findings.finding: The Binding Implementation Sequence mandates a two-phase CLI execution (singleton run first, record as pinned baseline; then packed run compared against that baseline), which is a distinct deliverable — yet no Acceptance Criterion explicitly requires this two-phase ordering to be implemented. AC 14 only checks that thresholds are met at final evaluation time, leaving the mandatory phasing unverifiable from the AC list alone.
findings.location: Binding Implementation Sequence steps 1–2 vs Acceptance Criteria AC 14
scenarios: Reviewer checks AC 14 as passing because the packed run alone meets the absolute 0.90/0.10 floors, even though the singleton baseline was never recorded and the relative comparison (packed ≥ singleton) was never enforced.
Implementer skips the singleton-first slice and jumps straight to packed evaluation; all ACs pass while a critical quality regression gate is missing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly what `min_gold` counts in the prerequisite context (e.g., 'the number of distinct `(gold_prerequisite_id, ItemRecord)` pairs, equal to the total number of prerequisite evaluations across all gold sets') and verify that 'eight sets of at least three' yields ≥20 by that definition.
evidence: 'prerequisite_fidelity_report(... min_gold: int = 20 ...)'
'Add eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites: two all-consistent, two single-conflict, two tail-conflict, one attribution-confusion, and one multi-bin'
No definition of what constitutes one 'item' counted by min_gold in the prerequisite context
findings.finding: The `prerequisite_fidelity_report` signature uses `min_gold: int = 20` as the minimum corpus size, but the gold corpus spec says 'eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites' (minimum 24 prerequisite evaluations). The plan never defines what unit `min_gold` counts — ItemRecord instances (which may be many per prerequisite), distinct canonical prerequisite IDs, or gold set count — leaving the executor unable to verify whether 8 sets of 3 prerequisites satisfies `min_gold=20`.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 3 (`prerequisite_fidelity_report` definition) and Implementation section ('Add eight or more gold sets')
scenarios: If min_gold counts ItemRecords and each gold set produces 3 records, 8 sets = 24 records > 20 → passes. If a consistent set produces 0 finding records, 8 sets with 2 consistent sets = 18 finding records < 20 → fails. The result depends on an unstated counting rule.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the `prerequisite_fidelity_report` spec: 'Here `min_gold` counts the number of `ItemRecord` entries in the candidate sequence, where each entry corresponds to one prerequisite evaluation; eight gold sets of three prerequisites each yields 24 entries, satisfying the default of 20.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether ItemRecord in the prerequisite fidelity context is per-prerequisite-slot (one per canonical ID evaluated) or per-finding-prediction, and reconcile the stated corpus size ('eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites') with the `min_gold=20` floor so both constraints are simultaneously satisfiable under the same counting rule.
evidence: 'Add eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites: two all-consistent, two single-conflict, two tail-conflict, one attribution-confusion, and one multi-bin.'
'prerequisite_fidelity_report(baseline: Sequence[ItemRecord], candidate: Sequence[ItemRecord], *, min_gold: float = 0.90, max_false_accept: float = 0.10, min_gold: int = 20)'
For all-consistent sets there are zero incompatibility findings; if ItemRecord maps to a finding-level prediction, those sets are invisible to the counter.
findings.finding: The `prerequisite_fidelity_report` function signature in Binding step 3 sets `min_gold=20`, but the Implementation section mandates only 'eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites'. For all-consistent gold sets (two mandated), no incompatibility findings exist — if ItemRecords are per-finding rather than per-prerequisite-slot, those sets contribute zero records, making the 20-record floor unachievable or dependent on an unstated counting convention. The plan never defines what constitutes one ItemRecord in the prerequisite context.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Add eight or more gold sets...') vs Binding Implementation Sequence step 3 ('min_gold: int = 20')
scenarios: Implementer writes the corpus with 8 sets × 0-3 findings, totalling fewer than 20 ItemRecords, and the `min_gold` floor triggers a spurious failure or is silently waived.
Implementer counts one ItemRecord per prerequisite-slot (24 records), passing the floor, while a different implementer counts per finding (possibly <20), causing disagreement.
findings.suggested_fix: Define that each (gold-set × prerequisite) pair produces exactly one ItemRecord regardless of finding count, making ItemRecord a coverage-slot record. This yields 8 × 3 = 24 ≥ 20 and counts consistent slots correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` as an edit target, specify the exact new signature for `_build_verification_output`, and confirm how `prerequisite_attribution_valid` reaches the `Binary` model without modifying `_build_binary` (e.g., a post-construction field injection or a separate `extra_veto_fields` dict passed to `create_model`).
evidence: Confirmed at `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py:108`: `def _build_verification_output(forbid: Any, severity_cls: type) -> type:` — two parameters only.
`plan_review_verification_model` currently calls `return _build_verification_output(forbid, PlanSeverityAttrs)` — no third argument.

The plan says 'Do not add the field to shared `_build_binary`' but `_build_verification_output` calls `_build_binary` internally — the extension point for an extra binary field is actually inside `_build_binary`, not just `_build_verification_output`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_build_verification_output` will be extended 'with an optional extra-binary-fields argument' so only `plan_review_verification_model` passes `prerequisite_attribution_valid`, but the current function signature `(forbid, severity_cls)` has no such parameter, and the plan never explicitly names extending this kernel function as a discrete edit step.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2. Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid`...'
scenarios: Implementor passes `extra_binary_fields` to `_build_verification_output` which then ignores it (not threaded to `_build_binary`), so `prerequisite_attribution_valid` never appears in the model.
The kernel function is in `review_kernel/verify_models.py` (shared with code-review); a modification there without careful scope risks contaminating code-review's `Binary` model.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `_build_verification_output` extra-binary-fields parameter: name it, give its type (e.g., `extra_binary_fields: dict[str, tuple] | None = None`), and state where/how the extra fields are merged into the Binary model construction.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid` with default `na` and vocabulary `yes|no|na`. `verification_model` and `code_review_verification_model` pass no extra field and remain schema-identical.'
Codebase: verify_models.py line 108 — current `_build_verification_output(forbid, severity_cls)` with no third parameter; line 91 `_build_binary(forbid)` builds the Binary model with fixed fields.
The plan does not name the parameter (e.g., `extra_binary_fields: dict | None = None`) or specify how fields are injected into the pydantic `create_model` call.
findings.finding: `_build_verification_output` is to be extended with an 'optional extra-binary-fields argument', but the plan never specifies the parameter's type, name, or merge protocol. The current function signature is `_build_verification_output(forbid, severity_cls)` and it calls `_build_binary(forbid)` which builds the shared Binary model. The plan says only `plan_review_verification_model` will pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid` via this argument; code-review and base verification pass nothing. An implementor needs to know: (a) what type the argument is (dict of field→FieldInfo? list of tuples?), (b) whether extra fields are merged into `_build_binary`'s output or appended to the Binary model after construction, and (c) whether the strict/non-strict (`forbid`) mode applies to the extra fields.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid` with default `na` and vocabulary `yes|no|na`'
scenarios: Implementor adds `prerequisite_attribution_valid` to `_build_binary` instead (affecting all gates) — a correctness error the plan explicitly forbids but gives no clear mechanism to avoid.
Two implementors independently choose incompatible signatures (dict vs kwargs vs list-of-tuples) making the extension non-interoperable.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the `mint_finding_id` encoding change applies only when `prerequisite_id` is present (leaving non-prerequisite IDs stable) or globally — and if globally, document the ID-stability break and any required migration or version-bump.
evidence: Current code: `basis = finding.get('finding', '') + '|' + ','.join(sorted(finding.get('criteria', [])))`; new encoding always appends a length-prefixed empty byte string even when `prerequisite_id` is absent
AC: 'Same-text findings from different prerequisite IDs retain distinct finding IDs' — this can also be satisfied by a dedicated prerequisite-finding mint function

Binding step 5: 'Extend `mint_finding_id` to use the authoritative structured identity encoding defined in Binding implementation sequence step 5 whenever `prerequisite_id` is present; identical text from different prerequisites cannot deduplicate.' — the 'whenever prerequisite_id is present' qualifier implies the change may be conditional, but the encoding spec (step 5) defines a single three-field encoding that always includes prerequisite_id or empty bytes, changing the hash even for absent cases
findings.finding: The plan extends `mint_finding_id` with the new length-prefixed encoding for ALL findings (including non-prerequisite ones, which get an appended empty-byte-string field), silently changing every existing finding's SHA-256 fingerprint. This breaks sidecar–coach cross-run referencing, novelty-match ID comparison, and any stored attestation finding ID that was computed before this change. The plan provides no rationale for why `mint_finding_id` must be modified globally rather than introducing a new `mint_prerequisite_finding_id` (or overloading only when `prerequisite_id` is present), which would satisfy the same-text/different-prerequisite deduplication AC without changing non-prerequisite IDs.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend `mint_finding_id` to use the authoritative structured identity encoding'; Binding implementation sequence step 5
scenarios: Any run after the change produces different IDs for all existing findings, so the coach step's `finding_refs`, the sidecar's stored finding IDs, and any novelty-matching cross-run comparisons that key on `id` will silently not match prior findings
A re-review of a ticket that was reviewed before this change will see all its findings as 'new' in novelty matching
findings.suggested_fix: Implement a dedicated `mint_prerequisite_finding_id(finding, prerequisite_id)` function using the length-prefixed encoding for focused findings, and leave `mint_finding_id` unchanged for general findings. This satisfies AC 'Same-text findings from different prerequisite IDs retain distinct finding IDs' without breaking existing sidecar/attestation finding IDs.

findings.finding: The plan states 'Python validation inside `plan_review_decide` requires complete arrays when `has_prerequisites=true`' without defining what 'complete' means — whether it requires all four optional fields to be present, non-null, or non-empty.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Python validation inside `plan_review_decide` requires complete arrays when `has_prerequisites=true`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback or back-out plan for the `finalize_verdict` modification — at minimum describe a feature-flag or expand-contract deploy strategy so the INDETERMINATE-priority branch can be disabled independently if the prerequisite preload proves unreliable in production.
evidence: 'immediately after extracting partition lists and before the current `if blocking` branch, add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"`, with existing branches changed to `elif`'
'This ordering intentionally implements the certified epic requirement that any indeterminate prerequisite makes the whole review unsigned INDETERMINATE, even when another finding would block.'
No rollback path, feature flag, or expand-contract sequence is mentioned for this modification.
findings.finding: `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` is explicitly modified to demote a previously-blocking verdict to INDETERMINATE whenever any prerequisite record is indeterminate — a behavioral change that affects all plan reviews that use prerequisite evaluation. The plan justifies the ordering but describes no rollback path, feature-flag, or expand-contract strategy if the prerequisite preload turns out to be systematically unreliable (e.g. a broken upstream pin interface causing mass-INDETERMINATE instead of mass-BLOCKED).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'This story explicitly modifies orchestrator.finalize_verdict'
scenarios: The upstream pin interface (`75de-02b0`) has an outage or returns malformed data; every plan review that touches prerequisites becomes INDETERMINATE, blocking the entire review pipeline with no way to revert to pre-modification behaviour.
A bug in the prerequisite preload path causes all records to return `evaluation-error`; the INDETERMINATE path fires universally and the only remedy is a code rollback with no described procedure.

findings.finding: The decision rule 'never `na` for a focused finding' for `prerequisite_attribution_valid` is stated in the focused verifier prose but is not co-located with the Binary field definition or the `plan_review_verification_model` factory that produces it. An executor reading only the field's description (`yes|no|na`) has no indication that `na` is prohibited for focused findings; the prohibition lives in a distant prose paragraph.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Set attribution `yes` only when … `no` for wrong or unprovable attribution, and never `na` for a focused finding'

findings.finding: The `prerequisite_attribution_valid` conversion rule — '`no` or `na` attribution converts that prerequisite record to `indeterminate/attribution-invalid` and removes its incompatibility findings' — is stated in `plan_review_decide` prose, but is not co-located with the Binary field definition or the `_build_verification_output` extension. An executor writing `plan_review_decide` must search across two sections to discover the veto semantics, which is the pink-elephant antipattern: the field's description (`yes|no|na`) gives no hint of the downstream veto conversion.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'In `plan_review_decide`, independently reshape and decide each domain … missing, duplicate, malformed, out-of-range, `no`, or `na` attribution converts that prerequisite record to `indeterminate/attribution-invalid`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract a shared `_greedy_bin_pack(items, item_charge_fn, fixed_cost, limit)` inner helper in sizing.py that both pack_container_bins and pack_prerequisite_bins delegate to, or explicitly confirm that the structural duplication is intentional and document why the two callers differ enough to preclude sharing.
evidence: pack_container_bins (sizing.py:48-76) uses: fixed_cost = parent_tokens, greedy append while fixed+sum(charges)<=budget, oversized for items whose fixed+item_alone>budget, returns (bins, oversized).

Binding step 4 specifies pack_prerequisite_bins with: fixed input = est_tokens(system_prompt)+est_tokens(subject_plan)+VERIFY_SYSTEM_RESERVE_TOKENS, greedy append while fixed+sum(charges)<=limit, oversized for items whose fixed+charge>limit alone, returns (bins, oversized). Structurally isomorphic.

Both functions live in sizing.py. Neither is designated an experiment/POC. There are currently 2 call sites for pack_container_bins (pass1.py:166, sizing.py:334), so Rule-of-Three for a shared generic packer is not yet met — but creating a parallel implementation rather than a shared helper increases future divergence risk without meeting R3 either.
findings.finding: pack_prerequisite_bins in sizing.py rebuilds the greedy bin-packing algorithm already implemented by pack_container_bins in the same file, rather than extracting a shared inner kernel that both call.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define pack_prerequisite_bins(blocks, *, subject_plan, system_prompt, model, per_block_output_tokens, headroom) in sizing.py' / Binding implementation sequence step 4 / sizing.py:48-76
scenarios: A future maintainer adjusts the greedy packing algorithm (e.g. adds a headroom safety margin or changes how oversized items are detected) and updates only one of the two copies, silently diverging prerequisite and container packing behavior.
A token-estimation bug is fixed in pack_container_bins but not in pack_prerequisite_bins (or vice versa), causing inconsistent context-limit behavior between the two paths.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a private `_greedy_pack(items: list[T], charge_fn: Callable[[T], int], fixed_cost: int, limit: int) -> tuple[list[list[T]], list[T]]` helper in sizing.py implementing the greedy-append + oversized-list logic once, then have both pack_container_bins and pack_prerequisite_bins call it with domain-specific charge functions and fixed-cost values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either confirm that `PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS` and `DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM` already exist in `sizing.py` (and reference their current values), or add their definitions to the Implementation section.
evidence: 'per_block_output_tokens: int = PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS' and 'headroom: float = DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM' — used as default values without definition
No 'Add constant X = Y to sizing.py' instruction appears in the Implementation section
findings.finding: The `pack_prerequisite_bins` function signature references `PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS` and `DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM` as already-existing named constants from `sizing.py`, but the plan neither defines their values nor confirms they already exist in that module. An executor implementing `pack_prerequisite_bins` as the first touch to `sizing.py` has no value to use.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 4 (`pack_prerequisite_bins` definition)
scenarios: If these constants do not yet exist, the executor must invent values, potentially causing inconsistency with the rest of the sizing system.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the key architectural decisions (verdict-override ordering, structured finding-identity encoding, prerequisite isolation boundary, independent domain separation in decide) in an ADR or AGENTS.md section so future maintainers can discover the rationale without reading the original ticket.
evidence: Plan describes verdict override: 'This ordering intentionally implements the certified epic requirement that any indeterminate prerequisite makes the whole review unsigned INDETERMINATE'
Plan describes new identity encoding: 'Use structured finding identity rather than printable concatenation: hash length-prefixed UTF-8 fields'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: Novel architectural decisions (independent decide-domain separation, structured finding-identity encoding, prerequisite isolation boundary, verdict-override ordering in `finalize_verdict`) are not captured in a durable design artifact such as an ADR or AGENTS.md update; the plan's in-ticket prose is not a discoverable record for future maintainers.
findings.location: Implementation section — orchestrator.finalize_verdict modification; Binding implementation sequence step 5; schema changes to plan_review_decide_input.schema.json
scenarios: A future maintainer modifying `finalize_verdict` does not know why the `prerequisite_indeterminate` branch must precede `if blocking` and removes or reorders it
A future contributor extends the identity encoding without realizing the SHA-256 field-ordering contract, causing silent deduplication collisions
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to `AGENTS.md` (or a new `docs/adr/prerequisite-review.md`) covering: (1) why `prerequisite_indeterminate` overrides `blocking` in `finalize_verdict`, (2) the SHA-256 length-prefixed field-ordering contract for finding identity, (3) the two non-overlapping index domains and why they must not merge before `partition_findings`, and (4) the isolation invariant that only focused prompts may receive prerequisite material.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the escalation cost policy for `models_at_or_above()` — either cap escalation to a named tier, emit a structured warning when escalation occurs, or add an operator-configurable `allow_escalation` flag — so that cost implications of large prerequisite blocks are visible and controllable.
evidence: 'Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`. Largest-window exhaustion emits `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=input-too-large`'
No mention of cost caps, model-tier limits, or operator-visible escalation warnings anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The model-escalation path (`models_at_or_above()`) during context-limit errors has no cost guardrail — a single oversized prerequisite block that exceeds all but the largest-window model could silently route to a much more expensive model with no budget ceiling, warning, or operator opt-out.
findings.location: Implementation section — sizing adapter and escalation policy: 'Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`. Largest-window exhaustion emits `indeterminate/evaluation-error`'
scenarios: A prerequisite block with a very long plan text causes a bin to exceed the default model's window, triggering escalation to a premium large-context model on every review containing that prerequisite
Repeated escalations across multiple review runs accumulate unnoticed cost

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability instrumentation (at minimum structured log entries, ideally counters/metrics) for: each evaluation-error and indeterminate disposition emitted, each context-limit escalation event (split level and model), each focused finder and verifier call outcome, and attribution-invalid occurrences.
evidence: Plan specifies context-limit splitting, model escalation, and provider-failure disposition paths but contains zero references to logging, metrics, tracing, or alerting.
ABSENCE: no `log`, `metric`, `trace`, `instrument`, `emit`, `counter`, or `alert` language appears anywhere in the plan.
New LLM call types introduced: focused finder (`plan_review_prerequisite_coverage`), focused verifier (reusing `plan_review_verification` contract) — neither has instrumentation specified.
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation is specified for any of the new failure points introduced by this plan: transient LLM provider failures, context-limit escalation events (each recursive split and each model upgrade attempt), evaluation-error dispositions emitted per unresolved ID, and indeterminate verdicts triggered by prerequisite failures. Operators have no way to detect elevated prerequisite evaluation failure rates, runaway escalation loops, or systematic provider issues affecting this path.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Size the actual focused prompt topology... On context-limit error, split a stable bin into contiguous halves recursively. Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`. Largest-window exhaustion emits `indeterminate/evaluation-error`...' and 'Non-context provider failure emits that record for every unresolved ID.'
scenarios: A transient provider outage causes all prerequisite reviews to silently become INDETERMINATE, surfacing only as verdict changes with no operational signal.
A misconfigured or oversized prerequisite block causes repeated context-limit escalations on every review call; the escalation loop is invisible until latency degrades.
Attribution-invalid dispositions accumulate on a bad prompt change; there is no metric to detect the regression before it affects many reviews.
findings.suggested_fix: In `passes.py`, after mapping unresolved IDs to `evaluation-error` or `indeterminate`, emit a structured log line (e.g., `logger.warning('prerequisite_evaluation_error', extra={'reason': reason_code, 'prerequisite_id': pid, 'detail': detail})`). In the sizing/escalation path, log each recursive split and each model escalation attempt. Consider a simple counter metric (e.g., `prerequisite_indeterminate_total{reason=...}`) so dashboards can alert on elevated rates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `.venv/bin/python` with a cross-platform invocation (e.g. `python -m pytest` with an activated venv, or `uv run pytest`) and replace `make check` with an equivalent platform-agnostic command or document a Windows alternative so the Verification section is executable on the Windows platform cell.
evidence: Plan text: 'Run .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py ...'
'Run make check and .venv/bin/python -m pytest -m "not integration"'
On Windows, Python venvs place the interpreter at .venv\Scripts\python.exe; .venv/bin/ does not exist, so the shell cannot find the executable.
GNU Make is not shipped with Windows; without WSL or a third-party install, `make` resolves to nothing and the shell returns a command-not-found error.
findings.finding: The Verification section hard-codes `.venv/bin/python` (a POSIX path) and `make check` (requires GNU Make) in every verification command. On Windows, the virtual-environment executable lives at `.venv\Scripts\python.exe` and `make` is not available by default, so every listed verification command fails before executing a single test.
findings.location: Verification section — all four bullet points (`Run .venv/bin/python -m pytest ...`, `Run make check`)
scenarios: Windows developer workstation: running any of the four verification commands verbatim produces 'No such file or directory' (.venv/bin/python) or ''make' is not recognized as an internal or external command' — the entire verification section is non-executable without manual adaptation.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `python -m pytest <args>` (assuming an activated venv or `uv run python -m pytest <args>`) throughout and replace `make check` with `python -m rebar_checks` or equivalent; add a note that `make check` requires GNU Make and provide the raw commands it wraps for Windows users.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `PlanRelationSnapshot` (including its fields `relation_kind`, `readable_state`, `pinned_material`, and ticket plan text) is importable from `src/` before implementation begins, OR add an explicit stub/interface-contract section defining its expected shape so the story can proceed in parallel.
evidence: Grep for `PlanRelationSnapshot` in src/ returns zero matches in implementation files.
All matches are in `.rebar/cache/plan-review/` — findings from prior reviews flagging the same absence.
Ticket 75de (the supplier) is not yet complete/certified in a way that makes this symbol importable.
The plan's 'This story neither recollects relations nor changes parent and child context handling' depends on this type existing — if it does not, this story has no input contract to build against.

findings.location: Upstream contract section: 'The pin interface supplies one review-time `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload keyed by authoritative canonical ticket ID, including relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and ticket plan text.'
scenarios: Developer begins implementing the prerequisite criterion and finds `PlanRelationSnapshot` undefined — the entire story's routing, isolation test, and packing logic have no typed input.
The isolation test `test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py` cannot assert 'one preload supplies both pin creation and prerequisite routing' without the type existing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an explicit plan step to extend `pass3_over_findings` with an `execution_review` parameter (and update the kernel's `decide.py`), or replace the call-site description with the actual mechanism (e.g., a `threshold_for` override that bakes the 0.8 floor) that achieves the same effect without a new parameter.
evidence: Confirmed signature at `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py:579-605`: `def pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs, *, threshold_for, impact_fn=None)` — no `execution_review` parameter.

The plan says 'applying the 0.8 execution floor consistently' implying this parameter gates a floor — its absence means the floor logic is unimplemented.

scenarios: Implementor calls `pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=True)` and gets a `TypeError: unexpected keyword argument` at runtime.
If the implementation instead extends the kernel signature without a plan step calling that out, a separate reviewer may not catch the undocumented kernel change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the custom 4-byte length-prefix binary identity encoding against established alternatives (e.g., RFC 8785 canonical JSON + SHA-256, or `json.dumps(sort_keys=True)` over a fixed field dict) and confirm no existing identity mechanism in the codebase already covers this case.
evidence: "encode each field as UTF-8, prefix it with a 4-byte big-endian unsigned byte length, and concatenate fields in this order: finding text, comma-joined sorted criteria, and authoritative prerequisite ID or the empty byte string. Hash the concatenated bytes with SHA-256"

"No pipe-delimited identity path remains." — the plan rejects one prior approach but does not compare the chosen approach against canonical JSON (RFC 8785) or hashlib-over-json-dumps(sort_keys=True), which already handle separator ambiguity without a custom binary framing.
findings.finding: The structured finding identity encoding (Binding implementation sequence step 5) specifies a custom binary format — 4-byte big-endian length-prefixed UTF-8 field concatenation fed to SHA-256 — without considering canonical alternatives such as RFC 8785 canonical JSON or stable sorted-key JSON serialization, both of which are library-supported and free of separator-ambiguity concerns without a bespoke serialization scheme.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence and evaluation contract, step 5
scenarios: A future implementer must maintain and document a non-standard binary serialization; any bug in length-prefix computation (off-by-one on multi-byte UTF-8) silently changes finding IDs across runs.
If the existing codebase already uses JSON-based hashing elsewhere, this introduces a second incompatible identity mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bespoke binary framing with a canonical JSON object `{"text": <finding_text>, "criteria": <sorted_criteria_list>, "prerequisite_id": <id_or_null>}` serialized with `json.dumps(sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=False).encode("utf-8")` then SHA-256 hashed. This is unambiguous, library-standard, human-readable in logs, and already handles the empty-prerequisite case without a custom length-prefix scheme.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the Pass-1 focused prerequisite finder's prompt library ID string (e.g., state `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` explicitly in the plan, matching the same naming pattern as `PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER`).
evidence: Plan quote: 'Add `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` and `PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER = "plan-review-prerequisite-verifier"` in `passes.py`'
The verifier string value is inferrable from the binding signal-chain section; the finder string is never stated anywhere in the plan.

Codebase: passes.py lines 230-241 show the pattern: PASS_FINDER='plan-review-finder', PASS_VERIFIER='plan-review-verifier', PASS_NOVELTY='plan-review-novelty' — each is a prompt-library lookup key that must exist as a prompt file.
ABSENCE: no 'plan-review-prerequisite-finder' string appears anywhere in the plan text.
findings.finding: The Pass-1 focused prerequisite prompt library ID string is never named. The plan adds `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` to passes.py (by analogy with `PASS_VERIFIER = "plan-review-prerequisite-verifier"` which IS named), but the plan only quotes the verifier's string value (`plan-review-prerequisite-verifier`); the finder's string is absent. Every existing prompt constant in passes.py maps to a named prompt-library ID (e.g., `PASS_FINDER = 'plan-review-finder'`, `PASS_VERIFIER = 'plan-review-verifier'`) that resolves via `prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, ...)`. The focused-finder prompt file ID is the one input `_resolve_system` needs that the plan never states.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER` and `PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER` in `passes.py`'; Binding signal-chain section: '`PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER = "plan-review-prerequisite-verifier"'`
scenarios: Implementor cannot create the focused-finder RunRequest without knowing the prompt library ID to pass to prompts.get_prompt().
If the implementor guesses 'plan-review-prerequisite-finder' they may be correct, but this is an undefined behavior the plan should not leave to guessing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'Add `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` in `passes.py`' to the Implementation section, mirroring the verifier constant declaration.

findings.finding: The `prerequisite_indeterminate → INDETERMINATE` override is inserted into `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` and driven by `coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')`. This value is computed in `plan_review_coach` from `prerequisite_coverage` input, which must be threaded from `plan_review_decide` output through the YAML wiring. The plan has no named end-to-end contract test or AC that specifically proves this YAML-threaded signal chain (`prerequisite_coverage` decide-output → `plan_review_coach` input → `coverage['prerequisite_indeterminate']` → `finalize_verdict`) fires correctly. If the YAML threading is incorrect or the field is absent, `prerequisite_indeterminate` is silently `False`, the INDETERMINATE override never fires, and an indeterminate prerequisite returns PASS — directly violating AC12.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Wire focused finder and verifier steps and arrays through both branches in src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml. In workflow_ops.plan_review_coach, build the existing local coverage dictionary with a new prerequisite_indeterminate key...'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC or Verification: 'A workflow integration test proves that an indeterminate prerequisite record in decide output propagates through both YAML branches to produce an unsigned INDETERMINATE verdict.'

findings.finding: The `mint_finding_id` hash-basis extension to 'length-prefixed UTF-8 fields' is specified in Binding Sequence step 5 but the exact length-prefix format is never defined. The current implementation hashes a string concatenation with `|` separators; the plan replaces this with 'hash length-prefixed UTF-8 fields for finding text, sorted criteria, and optional authoritative prerequisite ID'. The prefix format (e.g. 4-byte big-endian length + bytes, varint, `len:` ASCII prefix) is unspecified, making the hash value non-deterministic across implementations.
findings.location: Binding Sequence step 5 — 'Use structured finding identity rather than printable concatenation: hash length-prefixed UTF-8 fields'
findings.suggested_fix: Define the encoding as: for each field, encode its UTF-8 bytes, prepend a 4-byte big-endian unsigned integer of the byte length, concatenate all such prefixed fields in order (finding_text, criteria_bytes, prereq_id_bytes), then SHA-256.

findings.finding: The Implementation section and Binding Sequence step 5 contradict each other on the `mint_finding_id` hash construction: the Implementation says 'its hash basis appends `|prereq:<canonical-id>` when prerequisite_id is present' (separator-delimited concatenation), while Binding Sequence step 5 explicitly says 'Use structured finding identity rather than printable concatenation: hash length-prefixed UTF-8 fields.'
findings.location: Implementation section ('Extend mint_finding_id') vs. Binding Sequence step 5
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the phrase 'appends |prereq:<canonical-id>' from the Implementation section and replace it with a forward-reference: 'uses the structured length-prefixed hash defined in Binding Sequence step 5 with the optional prerequisite_id field populated.'

findings.finding: Binding-sequence point 5 introduces a new 'length-prefixed UTF-8 fields' hash-basis encoding scheme for mint_finding_id, diverging from the existing separator-concatenation pattern used by every other hash-basis computation in the codebase (mint_finding_id, _checkpoint_key, novelty id in sidecar.py, DET id in sidecar.py, manifest fingerprint — all use `field + '|' + field` concatenation). The plan's own Implementation section already states the correct, minimal fix: 'hash basis appends |prereq:<canonical-id> when prerequisite_id is present' — a single append consistent with the existing pattern. The length-prefix scheme in point 5 is a new encoding discipline for which no codebase evidence of separator-ambiguity bugs exists.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence point 5 — 'Use structured finding identity rather than printable concatenation: hash length-prefixed UTF-8 fields'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace point 5's 'length-prefixed UTF-8 fields' with the simpler approach already described in the Implementation body: extend mint_finding_id's basis to `finding + '|' + sorted_criteria + ('|prereq:' + prerequisite_id if prerequisite_id else '')`. This is collision-safe for same-text, different-prerequisite findings because the canonical-ID strings are distinct, and it is consistent with the existing separator pattern.

findings.finding: The plan introduces three new failure paths (`evaluation-error`, `output-invalid`, `attribution-invalid`) that force INDETERMINATE verdicts, but specifies no observability instrumentation (log lines, metrics, traces, or alerts) for these paths. Operators cannot distinguish a mass attribution-invalid storm from a provider outage from a prompt regression without manual log archaeology.
findings.location: Implementation section — reason_code enum definition; `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` modification; Acceptance Criteria — 'Any indeterminate prerequisite record forces top-level unsigned INDETERMINATE'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decide-step rule for `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` findings whose `prerequisite_id` does not match the record's `prerequisite_id`: specify whether the record is coerced to `indeterminate/output-invalid`, the mismatched findings are dropped, or another explicit fallback applies.
evidence: Plan quote: '`finding` requires findings carrying that canonical `prerequisite_id` and no reason code.'

Plan quote: 'Missing, duplicate, unknown-ID, parse-invalid, or schema-invalid output maps unresolved IDs in the bin to `output-invalid`' — this covers structural output failures but not a well-formed record with a wrong finding prerequisite_id.
ABSENCE: no rule is stated for a structurally-valid record where findings carry a different prerequisite_id than the record's own prerequisite_id field.
findings.finding: The processing rule for a `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` with `disposition='finding'` that contains findings whose `prerequisite_id` does not match the record's own `prerequisite_id` is unspecified. The plan says `finding` disposition 'requires findings carrying that canonical `prerequisite_id`', but it never states the decide-step's handling when the model violates this constraint — whether the record is coerced to `indeterminate/output-invalid`, the non-matching findings are silently dropped, or the whole bin's unresolved IDs are mapped to `output-invalid`. This is a co-location gap: the constraint is stated in the schema section but the enforcement rule belongs in the `plan_review_decide` focused-domain processing protocol.
findings.location: Implementation section: '`finding` requires findings carrying that canonical `prerequisite_id` and no reason code.'
scenarios: The focused-finder LLM emits a PrerequisiteCoverageRecord for prereq-A but places findings attributed to prereq-B inside it. The plan's schema constraint says this is invalid, but the decide step has no specified fallback for this case.
A test asserting 'wrong-attribution finding → indeterminate/output-invalid' vs 'wrong-attribution finding is dropped' would need to pick an unspecified behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that all `finalize_verdict` call sites other than `plan_review_coach` (P8-shortcircuit, drift_refresh, gate_dispatch) either pass `prerequisite_indeterminate=False` explicitly or confirm that absent key is the defined 'no prerequisites evaluated' safe default, and document this in the plan.
evidence: Plan quote: 'In `workflow_ops.plan_review_coach`, build the existing local `coverage` dictionary with a new `prerequisite_indeterminate` key computed as `any(record.disposition == "indeterminate")` from its `prerequisite_coverage` input, then pass that same dictionary through the existing `coverage=coverage` argument to `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`.'

Codebase: orchestrator.py lines 497-574 — finalize_verdict has 4 call sites in the codebase (workflow_ops line 209 for P8 short-circuit, workflow_ops line 557 in plan_review_coach, drift_refresh line 645, gate_dispatch lines 336/396/431). None currently build a `prerequisite_indeterminate` key.
The plan says `coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` — Python treats absent key as falsy, but this is a protocol decision the plan should affirm explicitly for the non-coach call sites.
findings.finding: The `prerequisite_indeterminate` coverage key injection path into `finalize_verdict` is under-specified for call sites other than `plan_review_coach`. The plan says to add `if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate'): verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` at the top of `finalize_verdict`'s branch chain. But `finalize_verdict` is called from four call sites: `plan_review_coach` (the plan does specify this one — it builds a `coverage` dict with the new key), `plan_review_precheck` (for P8-too-big short-circuit), `drift_refresh` (builds its own inline `coverage` dict), and `gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict`. The plan specifies the key is computed in `plan_review_coach` from `prerequisite_coverage` input, but never states what the other call sites must do — specifically whether absent key is the defined 'no indeterminate prerequisites' signal, or whether those call sites must explicitly pass `prerequisite_indeterminate: False`.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`: immediately after extracting partition lists and before the current `if blocking` branch, add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"'
scenarios: A future refactor of `drift_refresh` or `gate_dispatch` that does not set this key gets `False` by default — this may be correct behavior, but it is an implicit contract rather than a stated one.
The `plan_review_precheck` P8-shortcircuit path calls `finalize_verdict` before prerequisite evaluation runs — the plan never says this path is prerequisite-exempt.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the existing `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` call sites outside the main workflow path (P8-too-big in `workflow_ops.py`, `drift_refresh` in `orchestrator.py`) and confirm each is explicitly documented as prerequisite-coverage-free by design.
evidence: Plan states: 'immediately after extracting partition lists and before the current `if blocking` branch, add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"'

`workflow_ops.py:209` calls `orchestrator.finalize_verdict(pctx, parts, coaching=[], coverage={"det": det_cov, "llm_ran": False}, ...)` — the P8-too-big path, which never supplies `prerequisite_indeterminate`
`orchestrator.drift_refresh()` also calls `finalize_verdict` (via the local path at orchestrator.py ~line 600+)
None of these call sites are enumerated as 'known safe' consumers of the modified function
findings.finding: The plan names `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py` for new `test_prerequisite_indeterminate_signal_chain_*` tests but does not enumerate the existing call sites outside `workflow_ops.py` that call `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` — specifically the P8-too-big short-circuit path in `workflow_ops.py` itself (line 209) — which passes a minimal `coverage` dict without `prerequisite_indeterminate`. While this is backward-safe (`.get()` defaults to `None`), the plan does not acknowledge this unenumerated call site as a known, verified-safe consumer.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`...' and `workflow_ops.py` line 209
scenarios: A future reader adding a new call to `finalize_verdict` won't know whether `prerequisite_indeterminate` must be supplied, potentially silently suppressing INDETERMINATE for prerequisites on the new path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the plan: the P8-too-big precheck path and `drift_refresh` both call `finalize_verdict` without prerequisite context (by design — they bypass the LLM tier); document that `coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` evaluating `None` is the intended behavior for those paths.
